tested_predictions.hypothesis: XGBoost will outperform logistic regression by ≥7pp macro_f1 because the top two numeric MI features (Total_EMI_per_month MI=0.5465, Monthly_Inhand_Salary MI=0.5179) show negligible linear separability for EMI (F=1.04) and no |r|>0.8 among features, enabling tree splits to exploit non-linear signal that LR misses
tested_predictions.basis: MI concentration in EMI and salary + EMI’s F=1.04 + no high correlations

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global XGBoost SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥2× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary, because its mutual_information=0.5465 exceeds salary’s 0.5179 yet has near-zero linear separability (F=1.04) so trees gain more from non-linear splits

Delay_from_due_date will have the largest absolute coefficient in logistic regression (>0.75) because its ANOVA F=11471.89 is 6× higher than the next feature’s F and indicates strongest linear class separation

Missing indicator for Type_of_Loan will rank in the top 10 SHAP features for XGBoost (mean |SHAP| ≥ median SHAP) because its missingness (11.41%) correlates with target (max_target_dist_diff=0.1951) as an MNAR signal

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.75) because its conditional means on Total_EMI_per_month (μ=1399.65) and Monthly_Inhand_Salary (μ=4242.04) lie between Good and Poor, creating bidirectional confusion with a bias toward Standard→Poor
supported_conjectures.basis: EMI means: Good=1477.20, Std=1399.65, Poor=1363.93 + salary means: Good=5389.25, Std=4242.04, Poor=3375.31
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Per-class recall and confusion matrix at evaluate-models to confirm Standard→Poor rate vs other confusions

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Logistic regression will achieve Good recall ≥0.80 because Good’s class_means on Delay_from_due_date (μ=10.89) and Monthly_Inhand_Salary (μ=5389.25) are well separated from Standard and Poor, yielding one-sided misclassification only at the Good→Standard boundary
supported_conjectures.basis: Delay means: Good=10.89 vs Std=19.75 vs Poor=29.74 + Salary separation Good vs neighbors
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Good class recall and error rates at evaluate-models

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Tree-based models will assign negligible importance to Delay_from_due_date relative to EMI and salary in SHAP, despite its F=11471, because its low MI=0.1216 indicates predominantly linear signal that trees undervalue
supported_conjectures.basis: Delay MI=0.1216 vs EMI MI=0.5465 + low pairwise correlations
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Global SHAP ranking from tree models

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Logistic regression will underutilize Num_Credit_Inquiries (MI=0.1081, F=6.26) compared to tree models, resulting in ≥3pp lower class-wise lift for Poor class when removing it
supported_conjectures.basis: Moderate MI=0.1081 vs low F=6.26 suggests non-linear splits capture more than coefficient
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Hold-out ablation test and class-wise lift differences in evaluate-models

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The ratio Total_EMI_per_month/Monthly_Inhand_Salary will exceed EMI’s MI by ≥10% (from 0.5465 to >0.60) by capturing payment burden non-linearity, since EMI and salary have no |r|>0.8
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Engineer the ratio, recompute MI and compare SHAP importance

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Interaction term Num_Credit_Inquiries×Delay_from_due_date will surface a non-linear risk signal boosting MI by ≥50% over each feature alone, because both have moderate MI (0.1081 & 0.1216) and low correlation
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Add product feature, measure MI change and impact on SHAP

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Binning Total_EMI_per_month into quartiles will increase its F-statistic >500 by introducing piecewise linear separability, given its continuous distribution and EMI’s original F=1.04
exploratory_leads.basis: EMI F=1.04 + high skewness (7.1025) suggests non-linear class boundaries
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Create quartile bins, run ANOVA and compare F-statistics

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Encoding the missingness of Type_of_Loan as a binary flag will improve XGBoost macro_f1 by ≥2pp, exploiting MNAR pattern (missing_pct=11.4%, max_target_dist_diff=0.1951)
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Add missing flag, compare model performance in evaluate-models

eda_hypotheses.model_selection_prediction: XGBoost will achieve macro_f1 ≥0.82, outperforming logistic regression by ≥7pp, because high-MI features with low linear separability and no feature correlation favor boosted trees
eda_hypotheses.class_struggle_prediction: Standard will have the lowest recall (≤0.75) with Standard→Poor as the dominant confusion pair due to overlapping conditional means on EMI and salary

Good.diagnosis: Recall=0.8303, highest among classes. Confusion matrix shows Good samples misclassified as Standard (523) ~5× more often than as Poor (104), indicating the decision boundary between Good and Standard is particularly thin.
Good.confusion_flow: Good -> Standard (asymmetric, 5:1 ratio vs Good -> Poor)

Poor.diagnosis: Recall=0.7655. Poor samples are misclassified as Standard (804) ~1.5× more than as Good (539), suggesting feature overlap in the mid-risk region.
Poor.confusion_flow: Poor -> Standard (asymmetric, 1.5:1 ratio vs Poor -> Good)

Standard.diagnosis: Recall=0.6177, lowest of all classes. Errors split almost evenly to Poor (2096) and Good (1949) with a slight bias toward Poor, indicating Standard overlaps both neighbors.
Standard.confusion_flow: Standard -> Poor (slightly asymmetric, 1.07:1 vs Standard -> Good)

capacity_analysis.logistic_regression: Underfitting: Optuna chose C=0.0746 (heavy regularisation). CV macro_f1=0.6415 vs test=0.6537 (1.2pp gap) and ~4pp behind XGBoost, indicating linear model cannot capture nonlinear interactions.
capacity_analysis.random_forest: Well-fitted: max_depth=13, min_samples_split=6 yield CV macro_f1=0.6784 vs test=0.6837 (0.5pp gap). High capacity without overfitting; performance plateaued.
capacity_analysis.xgboost: Well-fitted with minimal generalisation gap: learning_rate=0.01596, max_depth=7, subsample/colsample regularisation. CV macro_f1=0.6701 vs test=0.6947 (+2.6pp) and train/val losses decrease in tandem, showing strong nonlinear signal capture and controlled overfitting.

confidence_analysis.summary: Logistic regression shows a narrow confidence gap (correct 67.1% vs wrong 64.7%, ~2.4pp), indicating low self-awareness. Random forest has a moderate gap (73.3% vs 64.6%, ~8.7pp), with Standard seeing ~11.5pp separation. XGBoost exhibits the widest gap (77.9% vs 66.7%, ~11.3pp), especially for Standard (16.4pp), enabling reliable confidence-based filtering.

training_diagnostics.learning_curve_interpretation: XGBoost training and validation logloss decrease almost in lockstep for ~1500 rounds, then training loss continues to fall while validation plateaus around 0.687, indicating slight late-stage overfitting. Early stopping or stronger regularisation on rounds could improve generalisation.

tested.actual: XGBoost macro_f1=0.6947 vs LR macro_f1=0.6537, gap=4.30pp (<7pp)

tested.original: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global XGBoost SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥2× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary, because its mutual_information=0.5465 exceeds salary’s 0.5179 yet has near-zero linear separability (F=1.04) so trees gain more from non-linear splits

Delay_from_due_date will have the largest absolute coefficient in logistic regression (>0.75) because its ANOVA F=11471.89 is 6× higher than the next feature’s F and indicates strongest linear class separation
tested.actual: No logistic regression coefficient data provided

supported.original: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.75) because its conditional means on Total_EMI_per_month (μ=1399.65) and Monthly_Inhand_Salary (μ=4242.04) lie between Good and Poor, creating bidirectional confusion with a bias toward Standard→Poor
supported.evidence: Standard recall=0.5802 (RF), 0.5853 (LR), 0.6177 (XGB), lowest of all classes and <0.75

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Misclassified Standard instances by XGBoost have predicted probabilities <0.65, allowing a confidence threshold to flag >50% of errors
XGBoost precision exceeds 90% when confidence >0.75, enabling high-precision subsets

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Good→Standard misclassifications concentrate at mid-range EMI values, indicating non-linear EMI effects
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Partial dependence plot of EMI for misclassified Good instances

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Adding EMI/Salary ratio as a feature will improve Standard recall by >2pp by capturing payment burden
exploratory_leads.basis: Symmetric Standard confusion with both neighbors
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Engineer EMI/Salary ratio and evaluate impact on recall

observations: Credit_Mix is the top driver in both SHAP (mean_abs_shap=0.5488, rank#1) and grouped PFI (importance_mean=0.1561, rank#1), and its ablation drops macro_f1 by 18.59pp, confirming real_signal.
Outstanding_Debt is SHAP rank#2 (mean_abs_shap=0.3536) but PFI rank#3 (importance_mean=0.0735), indicating it contributes via interactions beyond its marginal effect.
Interest_Rate is SHAP rank#3 (mean_abs_shap=0.2973) and PFI rank#2 (importance_mean=0.0844), showing moderate agreement but slight divergence in marginal vs interaction attribution.
Month appears SHAP rank#4 (mean_abs_shap=0.2435) but PFI rank#10 (importance_mean=0.0051) and its ablation changes macro_f1 by –0.29pp, marking it as a weak calendar shortcut.
insights: Credit_Mix dominates because it proxies aggregated credit behavior patterns the model cannot access individually, making it indispensable for class separation.
Month’s high SHAP but low PFI importance and negligible ablation impact reveal a spurious calendar shortcut that the model learned but does not contribute robustly to predictive power.
Outstanding_Debt’s higher SHAP vs PFI ranking suggests it drives predictions through interactions (e.g. with EMI or salary) that SHAP attributes to it locally but PFI disperses marginally.

feature_importance_consensus.interpretation: The model agrees on Credit_Mix, Outstanding_Debt and Interest_Rate as core features. SHAP-only features (Month, Num_Credit_Card) are either shortcuts or redundantly captured elsewhere, while PFI-only features (Delay_from_due_date, Total_EMI_per_month) contribute primarily through marginal effects that SHAP attributes to correlated interactions.

Credit_Mix.class_direction: As Credit_Mix increases from 0→2, Good probability rises and both Poor and Standard probabilities fall
Credit_Mix.pdp_ale_divergence: PDP and ALE agree on a steady push toward Good with better mixes

Outstanding_Debt.class_direction: Moderate increases in debt (around the mid grid) temporarily reduce Good probability and boost Poor before returning to baseline
Outstanding_Debt.pdp_ale_divergence: PDP shows a mid-range dip in Good that ALE does not, indicating correlation bias in the PDP at middle debt levels

Interest_Rate.class_direction: Low-to-mid rates have little effect, but rates above ~20% sharply increase Poor probability while reducing Good and Standard
Interest_Rate.pdp_ale_divergence: ALE shows a smoother transition than PDP’s sharper drop, suggesting PDP bias at extreme rates

cross_layer_validation.eda_to_xai: EDA predicted Total_EMI_per_month and Monthly_Inhand_Salary as top drivers (MI=0.5465,0.5179) but XAI ranks them 5th (SHAP=0.0754) and 12th (SHAP=0.0540), showing the model learned stronger categorical and interaction proxies (Credit_Mix, debt, rate) rather than raw numeric separations.
cross_layer_validation.training_to_xai: Training diagnostics flagged Standard as the most confused class; global XAI shows its overlapping feature effects (e.g. Credit_Mix centers and Outstanding_Debt thresholds) that blunt its separability, aligning with low recall and bidirectional confusions seen in the confusion matrix.
cross_layer_validation.fe_impact: No engineered interaction features were added; the model prioritized raw features, with engineered proxies (e.g. Num_Credit_Card) showing minor SHAP influence and low PFI impact, as expected under the FE hypothesis of a stable baseline.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Ablating Outstanding_Debt will reduce macro_f1 by more than 3pp
tested_predictions.basis: Outstanding_Debt’s high SHAP importance (0.3536) suggests critical predictive contribution
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Retrain or shuffle Outstanding_Debt and measure macro_f1 change

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Outstanding_Debt contributes primarily through interactions with other financial features rather than marginal effects
supported_conjectures.basis: SHAP rank#2 vs PFI rank#3 divergence
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Interaction SHAP dependence plots showing joint effects with EMI or salary

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Creating a Total_EMI_per_month to Monthly_Inhand_Salary ratio feature will improve Standard class recall by >2pp by directly capturing payment burden non-linearity
exploratory_leads.basis: Standard struggles due to overlapping EMI and salary distributions
exploratory_leads.implication: If true, ratio will yield a clearer separation for mid-risk Standard cases

Good.representative_profile: Row 4577: a prototypical Good case with very strong Credit_Mix (+1.1422 SHAP), many credit cards (+0.6221) and a moderate interest rate (+0.254) pushing confidently toward Good.
Good.borderline_story: Row 3859: low-confidence Good (0.3459) sits on the Good–Standard boundary where modest Credit_Mix (+0.634) is nearly canceled by low Annual_Income (−0.278) and high Total_EMI_per_month (−0.252), revealing income and EMI thresholds define the thin margin.
Good.worst_misclassification_story: Row 17916: true Good predicted as Standard (0.9032) because the model overweighs moderate Credit_Mix (+0.616 toward Standard), high Outstanding_Debt (+0.3999) and long Credit_History_Age (+0.3299), systematically biasing such profiles into Standard.

Poor.representative_profile: Row 2970: a clear Poor instance with very high Interest_Rate (+1.0526 SHAP), significant Delay_from_due_date (+0.342) and elevated Outstanding_Debt (+0.302) driving confident Poor predictions.
Poor.borderline_story: Row 12832: borderline Poor (0.3590) hinges on moderate Monthly_Inhand_Salary (+0.364) being offset by lower Outstanding_Debt (−0.272) and few Credit_Inquiries (−0.250), pinpointing salary and debt levels as the Poor–Standard dividing line.
Poor.worst_misclassification_story: Row 1304: true Poor misclassified as Standard (0.9265) because high Interest_Rate (+0.821 toward Standard), mid-level Credit_Mix (+0.524) and a late calendar Month (+0.423) overwhelm Poverty signals, indicating a systematic tilt toward Standard when rate and month are high.

Standard.representative_profile: Row 1321: classic Standard case with late Month (+0.990 SHAP), moderate Credit_Mix (+0.881) and elevated Outstanding_Debt (+0.770) jointly signaling Standard with high confidence.
Standard.borderline_story: Row 14582: low-confidence Standard (0.3532) sits on the Standard–Good boundary where weak Credit_Mix (−0.594) is nearly offset by modest Outstanding_Debt (+0.095) and interest rate (+0.084), flagging Credit_Mix as the fragile pivot.
Standard.worst_misclassification_story: Row 5976: true Standard predicted as Good (0.9681) because the model over-relies on very strong Credit_Mix (+1.136 toward Good), many credit cards (+0.651) and a small negative Month effect (−0.242 away), systematically misrouting such profiles into Good.

confusion_patterns.dominant_direction: Neighbors→Standard asymmetry: both Good→Standard (row 17916) and Poor→Standard (row 1304) are high-confidence misclassifications, showing the model biases toward Standard under uncertainty.

decision_boundary_analysis.thinnest_boundary: Good vs Standard: Annual_Income sits at a threshold in row 3859, with income ≈ boundary value triggering low-confidence Good.
decision_boundary_analysis.boundary_candidate_for_engineering: Engineer an EMI-to-income ratio (Total_EMI_per_month / Annual_Income) to sharpen the Good–Standard boundary, addressing the offsetting effects seen in row 3859.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: A confidence threshold of 0.90 would flag over 50% of high-confidence misclassifications (rows 17916, 1304, 5976).
tested_predictions.basis: All three worst misclassifications have confidences > 0.90.
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Compute coverage and precision of conf > 0.90 on misclassified instances.

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: High Outstanding_Debt systematically pushes true Good cases into Standard.
supported_conjectures.basis: Row 17916’s Outstanding_Debt (+0.3999 toward Standard) dominates Good cues.
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Distribution of debt levels for misclassified Good vs correctly classified Good.

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Rare occupation categories (e.g. architect) create spurious Standard signals leading to Good→Standard errors.
exploratory_leads.basis: Row 17916’s Occupation_architect (+0.0664 toward Standard) appears among top drivers.
exploratory_leads.implication: Collapsing low-frequency occupations may reduce this failure mode.

feature_engineering_hypothesis.interactions_rationale: Deterministic feature engineering: no new interactions are added; the priority is to preserve validated preprocessing features and unblock model training.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.dropped_features_rationale: No features are dropped by the fallback.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.expected_impact: Expected to be a stable baseline, possibly lower-performing than successful LLM FE, but preferable to failing the end-to-end pipeline.

selection_justification: Selected xgboost based on stronger macro_f1 and weighted_f1. LLM rationale: XGBoost achieved the highest accuracy (0.69925) and macro_f1 score (0.6947) among the models, indicating strong overall performance. It also handled the 'Good' and 'Poor' classes better than other models, with a balance of precision and recall. Despite a slight CV-test gap (0.6701 CV to 0.69925 test accuracy), this model shows a good generalization ability compared to the random forest. The balanced performance across classes and metrics supports its selection.
